verses.commentary: In the same way: following Christ's example of submission (2:21-23). Wives submit (hypotassō, ὑποτάσσω) to husbands. If husbands do not believe, they may be won without words by wives' behavior. Witness is conduct, not argument. Silent testimony speaks volumes.

Purity (hagnos, ἁγνός) and reverence (phobos, φόβος): holy conduct, not manipulation. Husbands observe lives, not lectures. Actions validate or invalidate words. Christlike character is the most compelling evangelism.

Beauty should not be external: elaborate hairstyles, gold jewelry, fine clothes. Not a prohibition of adornment but a priority statement. External beauty is temporary; godly character endures. Culture obsesses over appearance; God values the heart.

True beauty is the inner self (kryptos tēs kardias anthrōpos, κρυπτὸς τῆς καρδίας ἄνθρωπος): the hidden person of the heart. Unfading beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit. Gentle (praus, πραΰς): meek, humble. Quiet (hēsychios, ἡσύχιος): tranquil, settled. This is precious to God.

Holy women of the past adorned themselves this way. They hoped in God and submitted to husbands. Faith and submission linked. Trust in God's sovereignty enables submission in relationships. Historical examples validate the teaching.

Sarah called Abraham 'lord' (Genesis 18:12). You are her daughters by imitating her faith. Do what is right and do not fear (phobeo, φοβέω): give way to panic or intimidation. Righteous conduct requires courage, not fear of consequences.

Husbands, in the same way: follow Christ's example. Be considerate (kata gnōsin, κατὰ γνῶσιν): according to knowledge, understanding. Treat wives with respect as weaker partner: physically, not morally or spiritually. Heirs together of grace. Mutual inheritance in Christ. Relationship with wife affects relationship with God: prayers may be hindered.

Finally: summarizing relational ethics. Be like-minded (homophron, ὁμόφρων): united in thought. Sympathetic (sympathēs, συμπαθής): suffering together. Love one another (philadelphos, φιλάδελφος): brotherly love. Compassionate (eusplanchnos, εὔσπλαγχνος): tender-hearted. Humble (tapeinophron, ταπεινόφρων): lowly in mind. Unity without uniformity.

Do not repay evil with evil or insult with insult. Repay evil with blessing. Called to inherit a blessing, not retaliate. Breaking the cycle of vengeance requires supernatural grace. Blessing enemies reflects God's character (Matthew 5:44).

Psalm 34:12-16 quoted. Whoever loves life and wants good days must keep tongue from evil and lips from deceitful speech. Life and blessing are tied to righteous speech. The tongue reveals the heart (James 3:1-12).
Turn from evil, do good. Seek peace and pursue it (diōkō, διώκω): chase, hunt. Peace does not happen by accident; it requires intentional effort. Active pursuit of reconciliation and harmony.

The Lord's eyes are on the righteous, ears attentive to prayer. His face is against evildoers. God sees, hears, and responds. He is not neutral. Righteousness attracts God's favor; evil invites His opposition.

Who will harm you if you are eager (zēlōtēs, ζηλωτής) for good? Rhetorical question. Generally, goodness disarms hostility. But the next verse introduces the exception: sometimes righteousness provokes persecution.

But even if you suffer for righteousness, you are blessed (makarios, μακάριος). Isaiah 8:12-13 echoed: do not fear their threats. Blessing in suffering is paradoxical. Fear of man is displaced by fear of God.

In your hearts revere (hagiazō, ἁγιάζω) Christ as Lord: set apart, sanctify. Always be prepared (hetoimos, ἕτοιμος) to give a defense (apologia, ἀπολογία) for the hope you have. Christian witness requires readiness and reason. But do this with gentleness (prautēs, πραΰτης) and respect (phobos, φόβος). Truth without love is harsh; love without truth is weak.

Keep a clear conscience (syneidēsis agathē, συνείδησις ἀγαθή). Those who slander your good behavior in Christ may be ashamed. Integrity disarms accusers. A guilty conscience weakens witness; a clear conscience strengthens it.

Better to suffer for doing good, if it is God's will, than for doing evil. Suffering for righteousness honors God. Suffering for sin is deserved. The Christian distinction is the cause of suffering, not its presence.

Christ suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous. Substitutionary atonement: He took our place. Purpose: to bring you to God (prosagō, προσάγω). He was put to death in the body (sarx, σάρξ) but made alive in the Spirit (pneuma, πνεῦμα). Resurrection vindicates His sacrifice.

After being made alive, He went and proclaimed (kēryssō, κηρύσσω) to imprisoned spirits. Difficult text: likely refers to Christ's proclamation of victory over fallen angels or spirits of Noah's generation (Genesis 6:1-4). Not a second chance for salvation but declaration of judgment or triumph.

Those who were disobedient in Noah's days when God waited patiently. Eight people saved through water. Noah's ark is a type of salvation: judgment (flood) and rescue (ark). Few believed; most perished. Narrow is the way (Matthew 7:13-14).

Water symbolizes baptism that now saves. Not removal of dirt (physical washing) but pledge (eperōtēma, ἐπερώτημα) of a clear conscience toward God. Baptism does not save by the act itself but by what it represents: union with Christ's death and resurrection. It saves through the resurrection of Jesus Christ.

Christ has gone into heaven, at God's right hand. Angels, authorities, and powers are in submission to Him. Total cosmic authority. All spiritual forces bow to the risen, exalted Christ. He reigns supreme (Ephesians 1:20-23).
